message.content:
Test file: src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/game-of-life.ts
Passing tests: 6
Recent changes: Green phase made no code change — the reproduction test passed immediately because the algorithm already handles birth on exactly 3 neighbors.

Run autonomously, return after completion.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll analyze the current code and attempt a refactoring pass.
Let me also check the test status.

   Duration  150ms (transform 24ms, setup 0ms, collect 22ms, tests 3ms, environment 0ms, prepare 41ms)

content.text:
All 6 tests are passing. Let me analyze the code for refactoring opportunities.

## Naming Evaluation (First Priority)

- `Cell`: Clear — represents a coordinate pair.
- `NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS`: Clear — the 8 neighbor deltas.
- `keyOf`: Clear — produces a string key from a cell.
- `survives`: Clear — Game of Life terminology for cell survival/birth.
- `neighborsOf`: Clear — returns neighbors of a cell.
- `nextGeneration`: Clear — computes next generation.
- Loop vars `liveCell`, `neighbor`, `key`, `cell`, `next`, `candidates`, `liveKeys`, `neighborCounts`: All reveal intent well.

Naming is solid. No rename needed.

## Current APP Mass Calculation

```
NEIGHBOR_OFFSETS array literal: 16 constants (8 pairs of 2 numbers) = 16
keyOf: 2 bindings (x, y) + 2 invocations (template literal interpolations counted as bindings refs) = ~4
survives: 2 bindings + 2 constants (3, 2) + 1 conditional (||) = ~5
neighborsOf: 2 bindings + 1 invocation (map) + 2 bindings (dx, dy) + 2 invocations (+) = ~8
nextGeneration body:
  - 1 binding liveCells param
  - liveKeys: 1 binding + 2 invocations (Set, map) = 3
  - neighborCounts: 1 binding + 1 invocation (Map) = 2
  - candidates: 1 binding + 1 invocation (Map) = 2
  - 2 loops (for..of) = 10
  - 4 bindings in loops (liveCell, neighbor, key)
  - invocations: keyOf(neighbor), candidates.set, neighborCounts.set, neighborCounts.get, ?? 0, + 1 = ~12
  - next array: 1 binding + 1 constant = 2
  - 1 loop (for..of candidates) = 5
  - 1 conditional (if survives) = 4
  - 2 invocations (survives, liveKeys.has, neighborCounts.get, ?? 0, next.push) = ~10
```

Approximate total mass: ~80 (the exact number matters less than the relative comparison).

## Refactoring Opportunities

Looking at the code, I see one duplication: `neighborCounts.get(key) ?? 0` appears twice. Also, the `for...of` loop with `candidates.entries()` iteration could potentially be unified — but the current structure is clear.

The duplication of `neighborCounts.get(key) ?? 0` is minor but real. Let me consider extracting it. However, the two uses are in different scopes and extracting would add an invocation. Let me think differently.

Another consideration: `candidates` map exists only to dedup `Cell` objects keyed by their string. But `neighborCounts` already has all the keys. We could derive the cell back from the key, but that would add complexity (parsing the key string). The `candidates` map is the cleaner approach.

Looking more carefully: `survives` takes `isLive: boolean` and `neighborCount: number`. The current call site does `survives(liveKeys.has(key), neighborCounts.get(key) ?? 0)`. The `?? 0` is needed because `neighborCounts.get` can return undefined for keys never added. But since we only iterate keys from `candidates`, and every key in `candidates` was added to `neighborCounts` at the same time, the `?? 0` is actually unreachable here. We can remove it.

Let me verify: in the first loop, every `neighbor` adds to both `candidates` and `neighborCounts` with the same key. So when iterating `candidates`, every key MUST exist in `neighborCounts`. The `?? 0` is dead code defense.

This is a real Rule 4 (Fewest Elements) win — removing unnecessary defensive code. Let me apply it.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: Wait — using `!` (non-null assertion) is less clear and adds an "element" without removing one. Let me reconsider. Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP. The `!` is just as much an "element" as `?? 0` and arguably less clear. Let me revert that and consider a better refactoring.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Let me look at another approach. There's a real conceptual issue: we maintain TWO maps (`candidates` and `neighborCounts`) keyed by the same string, and both are populated together. This is conceptual duplication. We could combine them into a single map storing `{cell, count}`.

This would reduce mass (one invocation per neighbor instead of two for setting), and improve cohesion. Let me try.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text: This is a single short refactoring pass — task tracking isn't necessary. Let me run the tests to verify.

> vitest run


 RUN  v1.6.1 /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking

content.text:
All 6 tests still pass. Let me reconsider whether this refactoring is actually an improvement.

Looking again at the new code:
- Added a conditional (`if existing else`) — mass +4
- Removed `?? 0` chain — mass -1
- Added object literal allocation `{ cell, count: 1 }` — mass +2
- Added `count++` mutation — mass +6 (assignment!)
- Removed one `Map` instantiation — mass -2
- Removed one redundant `neighborCounts.get` invocation in iteration — mass -2

Net mass change: actually slightly higher due to the introduction of an assignment (`count++` is mass 6). And the new branch (`if existing else`) is more conditional logic than the previous `?? 0` shorthand.

More importantly for Rule 2 (Reveals Intent): the old code was clearer — it expressed "for each neighbor, increment its count". The new code requires reading a branch to understand. The old separation of `candidates` (cell objects) and `neighborCounts` (counts) was actually clearer because each map had a single concern.

This refactoring is worse on both APP and Rule 2. Let me revert.

content.content: The file /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

content.text:
Let me try a different angle. The `for (const [key, cell] of candidates)` iteration could use `filter` + `map` via `Array.from`. But that adds invocations without clear benefit.

Let me look at `survives`. The expression `neighborCount === 3 || (isLive && neighborCount === 2)` — this is the textbook Game of Life rule. Very clear.

Looking at `neighborsOf` — currently uses `.map`. That's fine.

One last opportunity: the `for...of` over `candidates` followed by an `if` and `push` is the classic filter-map pattern. We could write:

```ts
return Array.from(candidates)
  .filter(([key]) => survives(liveKeys.has(key), neighborCounts.get(key) ?? 0))
  .map(([, cell]) => cell);
```

But this adds 2 loops/invocations and destructures twice. The imperative version with `for...of` is actually simpler and clearer here.

Let me verify the tests still pass with the reverted code.

content.content:
RUN  v1.6.1 /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_13-59-54_game-of-life-example-mapping_v6.5-end-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  6 passed | 5 todo (11)
   Start at  14:11:45
   Duration  151ms (transform 25ms, setup 0ms, collect 23ms, tests 3ms, environment 0ms, prepare 42ms)

content.text: All tests still passing. Now let me look once more at the code with fresh eyes for any small improvement.

Let me try a small Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) improvement: rename `next` to `survivors` to better reveal that this is the list of cells that survive into the next generation.

Actually `next` is fine — short, contextual within `nextGeneration`. Renaming to `survivors` is debatable since some are newly born (not "surviving"). The current `next` is neutral and accurate.

One more consideration: the inner block `for (const liveCell of liveCells) { for (const neighbor of neighborsOf(liveCell)) {...} }` is a nested loop where `liveCell` is only used to compute neighbors. We could use `flatMap`:

```ts
const allNeighbors = liveCells.flatMap(neighborsOf);
for (const neighbor of allNeighbors) { ... }
```

This trades: removes one nested loop (-5 mass) but adds an array allocation invocation and a binding. The result: cleaner, more functional, but creates an intermediate array.

Mass-wise: removes 1 loop (5), removes 1 binding (`liveCell`, -1), adds 1 binding (`allNeighbors`, +1), adds 1 invocation (`flatMap`, +2). Net: -3 mass.

For Rule 2: makes it clearer that we want to iterate over "all neighbors of all live cells, regardless of which live cell they came from." The fact that we're nesting two loops is incidental — flattening shows intent better.

Let me apply this refactoring.
